solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
We represent a Dyck $n$-path by a sequence $a_1\cdots a_{2n}$, where
each $a_i$ is either $(1,1)$ or $(1,-1)$.

Given an $(n-1)$-path $P=a_1\cdots a_{2n-2}$, we distinguish two cases.
If $P$ has no returns of even-length, then let $f(P)$ denote the $n$-path
$(1,1)(1,-1)P$.  Otherwise, let $a_ia_{i+1}\cdots a_{j}$ denote the
rightmost even-length return in $P$, and let $f(P)=(1,1)a_1a_2\cdots
a_j(1,-1)a_{j+1}\cdots a_{2n-2}$.  Then $f$ clearly maps the set of Dyck
$(n-1)$-paths to the set of Dyck $n$-paths having no even return.

We claim that $f$ is bijective; to see this, we simply construct the
inverse mapping.  Given an $n$-path $P$, let $R=a_ia_{i+1}...a_j$
denote the leftmost return in $P$, and let $g(P)$ denote the
path obtained by removing $a_1$ and $a_j$ from $P$.  Then evidently
$f \circ g$ and $g \circ f$ are identity maps, proving the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Let $C_n$ be the number of Dyck paths of length $n$, let $O_n$ be the number
of Dyck paths whose final return has odd length, and let $X_n$ be the number
of Dyck paths with no return of even length.

We first exhibit a recursion for $O_n$; note that $O_0 = 0$.
Given a Dyck $n$-path
whose final return has odd length, split it just after its next-to-last return.
For some $k$ (possibly zero), this yields
a Dyck $k$-path, an upstep, a Dyck $(n-k-1)$-path whose odd return
has even length, and a downstep. Thus for $n \geq 1$,
\[
O_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} C_k (C_{n-k-1} - O_{n-k-1}).
\]

We next exhibit a similar recursion for $X_n$; note that $X_0 = 1$.
Given a Dyck $n$-path with no even return,
splitting as above yields for some $k$
a Dyck $k$-path with no even return,
an upstep, a Dyck $(n-k-1)$-path whose final return has even length,
then a downstep. Thus for $n \geq 1$,
\[
X_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} X_k (C_{n-k-1} - O_{n-k-1}).
\]

To conclude, we verify that $X_n = C_{n-1}$ for $n \geq 1$,
by induction on $n$. This is
clear for $n=1$ since $X_1 = C_0 = 1$. Given $X_k = C_{k-1}$ for $k<n$, we have
\begin{align*}
X_n  &=
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} X_k (C_{n-k-1} - O_{n-k-1}) \\
&= C_{n-1} - O_{n-1} + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} C_{k-1} (C_{n-k-1} - O_{n-k-1}) \\
&= C_{n-1} - O_{n-1} + O_{n-1} \\
&= C_{n-1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Note:}
Since the problem only asked about the \emph{existence} of a
one-to-one correspondence, we believe that any proof, bijective or not,
that the two sets
have the same cardinality is an acceptable solution. (Indeed, it would be
highly unusual to insist on using or not using a specific proof technique!)
The second solution
above can also be phrased in terms of generating functions.
Also, the $C_n$ are well-known to equal the Catalan numbers
$\frac{1}{n+1} \binom{2n}{n}$; the problem at hand is part of a famous
exercise in Richard Stanley's \textit{Enumerative Combinatorics, Volume 1}
giving 66 combinatorial interpretations of the Catalan numbers.

descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
We represent a Dyck $steppairs$-path by a sequence stepone\cdots steptwopairs, where
each stepi is either $(1,1)$ or $(1,-1)$.

Given an $(steppairs-1)$-path givenpath=stepone\cdots step_{2steppairs-2}, we distinguish two cases.
If givenpath has no returns of even-length, then let forwardmap(givenpath) denote the $steppairs$-path
$(1,1)(1,-1)$givenpath.  Otherwise, let stepi stepisuccessor\cdots stepj denote the
rightmost even-length return in givenpath, and let forwardmap(givenpath)=(1,1)stepone step_2\cdots
stepj(1,-1)step_{rightindex+1}\cdots step_{2steppairs-2}.  Then forwardmap clearly maps the set of Dyck
$(steppairs-1)$-paths to the set of Dyck $steppairs$-paths having no even return.

We claim that forwardmap is bijective; to see this, we simply construct the
inverse mapping.  Given a $steppairs$-path givenpath, let leftreturn=stepi stepisuccessor...stepj
denote the leftmost return in givenpath, and let invmap(givenpath) denote the
path obtained by removing stepone and stepj from givenpath.  Then evidently
forwardmap \circ invmap and invmap \circ forwardmap are identity maps, proving the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Let catalann be the number of Dyck paths of length steppairs, let oddreturn be the number
of Dyck paths whose final return has odd length, and let noevenret be the number
of Dyck paths with no return of even length.

We first exhibit a recursion for oddreturn; note that oddreturnzero = 0.
Given a Dyck steppairs-path
whose final return has odd length, split it just after its next-to-last return.
For some splitindex (possibly zero), this yields
a Dyck splitindex-path, an upstep, a Dyck $(steppairs-splitindex-1)$-path whose odd return
has even length, and a downstep. Thus for steppairs $\geq 1$,
\[
oddreturn = \sum_{splitindex=0}^{steppairs-1} catalank (catalanrem - oddremseg).
\]

We next exhibit a similar recursion for noevenret; note that noevenzero = 1.
Given a Dyck steppairs-path with no even return,
splitting as above yields for some splitindex
a Dyck splitindex-path with no even return,
an upstep, a Dyck $(steppairs-splitindex-1)$-path whose final return has even length,
then a downstep. Thus for steppairs $\geq 1$,
\[
noevenret = \sum_{splitindex=0}^{steppairs-1} noevenindex (catalanrem - oddremseg).
\]

To conclude, we verify that noevenret = $C_{steppairs-1}$ for steppairs $\geq 1$,
by induction on steppairs. This is
clear for steppairs=1 since noevenone = catalanzero = 1. Given noevenindex = $C_{splitindex-1}$ for splitindex<steppairs, we have
\begin{align*}
noevenret  &=
\sum_{splitindex=0}^{steppairs-1} noevenindex (C_{steppairs-splitindex-1} - oddremseg) \\
&= C_{steppairs-1} - oddreturn + \sum_{splitindex=1}^{steppairs-1} C_{splitindex-1} (C_{steppairs-splitindex-1} - oddremseg) \\
&= C_{steppairs-1} - oddreturn + oddreturn \\
&= C_{steppairs-1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Note:}
Since the problem only asked about the \emph{existence} of a
one-to-one correspondence, we believe that any proof, bijective or not,
that the two sets
have the same cardinality is an acceptable solution. (Indeed, it would be
highly unusual to insist on using or not using a specific proof technique!)
The second solution
above can also be phrased in terms of generating functions.
Also, the $C_{steppairs}$ are well-known to equal the Catalan numbers
$\frac{1}{steppairs+1} \binom{2steppairs}{steppairs}$; the problem at hand is part of a famous
exercise in Richard Stanley's \textit{Enumerative Combinatorics, Volume 1}
giving 66 combinatorial interpretations of the Catalan numbers.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
We represent a Dyck $sunflower$-path by a sequence $firetruck\cdots typewriter$, where
each $screwdriver$ is either $(1,1)$ or $(1,-1)$.

Given an $(sunflower-1)$-path $encyclopedia=firetruck\cdots thermometer_{2sunflower-2}$, we distinguish two cases.
If $encyclopedia$ has no returns of even-length, then let $\heliumgas(encyclopedia)$ denote the $sunflower$-path
$(1,1)(1,-1)\,encyclopedia$.  Otherwise, let $screwdriver_{armadillo}screwdriver_{armadillo+1}\cdots paperclip$ denote the
rightmost even-length return in $encyclopedia$, and let
\[
\heliumgas(encyclopedia)=(1,1)firetruck\,thermometer_2\cdots
paperclip(1,-1)thermometer_{blueberry+1}\cdots thermometer_{2sunflower-2}.
\]
Then $\heliumgas$ clearly maps the set of Dyck $(sunflower-1)$-paths to the set of Dyck $sunflower$-paths having no even return.

We claim that $\heliumgas$ is bijective; to see this, we simply construct the
inverse mapping.  Given a $sunflower$-path $encyclopedia$, let $marshmallow=screwdriver_{armadillo}screwdriver_{armadillo+1}\ldots paperclip$
denote the leftmost return in $encyclopedia$, and let $\hippodrome(encyclopedia)$ denote the
path obtained by removing $firetruck$ and $paperclip$ from $encyclopedia$.  Then evidently
$\heliumgas \circ \hippodrome$ and $\hippodrome \circ \heliumgas$ are identity maps, proving the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Let $raincoat$ be the number of Dyck paths of length $sunflower$, let $snowshoes$ be the number
of Dyck paths whose final return has odd length, and let $alligator$ be the number
of Dyck paths with no return of even length.

We first exhibit a recursion for $snowshoes$; note that $goldfish = 0$.
Given a Dyck $sunflower$-path whose final return has odd length, split it just after its next-to-last return.
For some $crocodile$ (possibly zero), this yields a Dyck $crocodile$-path, an upstep, a Dyck $(sunflower-crocodile-1)$-path whose odd return
has even length, and a downstep. Thus for $sunflower \ge 1$,
\[
snowshoes = \sum_{crocodile=0}^{sunflower-1} tortoise\,(toothbrush - blackboard).
\]

We next exhibit a similar recursion for $alligator$; note that $boomerang = 1$.
Given a Dyck $sunflower$-path with no even return,
splitting as above yields for some $crocodile$ a Dyck $crocodile$-path with no even return, an upstep, a Dyck $(sunflower-crocodile-1)$-path whose final return has even length, then a downstep. Thus for $sunflower \ge 1$,
\[
alligator = \sum_{crocodile=0}^{sunflower-1} kangaroo\,(toothbrush - blackboard).
\]

To conclude, we verify that $alligator = C_{sunflower-1}$ for $sunflower \ge 1$, by induction on $sunflower$. This is clear for $sunflower=1$ since $jellyfish = pinecone = 1$. Given $kangaroo = hairbrush$ for $crocodile<sunflower$, we have
\begin{align*}
alligator &= \sum_{crocodile=0}^{sunflower-1} kangaroo\,(toothbrush - blackboard) \
&= C_{sunflower-1} - snowshoes_{sunflower-1} + \sum_{crocodile=1}^{sunflower-1} hairbrush\,(toothbrush - blackboard) \\
&= C_{sunflower-1} - snowshoes_{sunflower-1} + snowshoes_{sunflower-1} \\
&= C_{sunflower-1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Note:}
Since the problem only asked about the \emph{existence} of a one-to-one correspondence, we believe that any proof, bijective or not, that the two sets have the same cardinality is an acceptable solution. The second solution above can also be phrased in terms of generating functions. Also, the $C_n$ are well-known to equal the Catalan numbers $\frac{1}{sunflower+1} \binom{2sunflower}{sunflower}$; the problem at hand is part of a famous exercise in Richard Stanley's \textit{Enumerative Combinatorics, Volume 1} giving 66 combinatorial interpretations of the Catalan numbers.

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
We represent a Dyck $microsize$-path by a sequence $lastleap\cdots firstleap$, where
each $finalstride$ is either $(1,1)$ or $(1,-1)$.

Given an $(microsize-1)$-path $emptypath=lastleap\cdots a_{2microsize-2}$, we distinguish two cases.
If $emptypath$ has no returns of even-length, then let $obliterate(emptypath)$ denote the $microsize$-path
$(1,1)(1,-1)emptypath$.  Otherwise, let $finalstridemiddlestride\cdots startingstride$ denote the
rightmost even-length return in $emptypath$, and let $obliterate(emptypath)=(1,1)lastleap a_2\cdots
startingstride(1,-1)a_{j+1}\cdots a_{2microsize-2}$.  Then $obliterate$ clearly maps the set of Dyck
$(microsize-1)$-paths to the set of Dyck $microsize$-paths having no even return.

We claim that $obliterate$ is bijective; to see this, we simply construct the
inverse mapping.  Given an $microsize$-path $emptypath$, let $departure=finalstridemiddlestride...startingstride$
denote the leftmost return in $emptypath$, and let $obstacle(emptypath)$ denote the
path obtained by removing $lastleap$ and $startingstride$ from $emptypath$.  Then evidently
$obliterate \circ obstacle$ and $obstacle \circ obliterate$ are identity maps, proving the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Let $chaosnumber$ be the number of Dyck paths of length $microsize$, let $evencounter$ be the number
of Dyck paths whose final return has odd length, and let $evenabound$ be the number
of Dyck paths with no return of even length.

We first exhibit a recursion for $evencounter$; note that $evencounterzero = 0$.
Given a Dyck $microsize$-path
whose final return has odd length, split it just after its next-to-last return.
For some $gigantindex$ (possibly zero), this yields
a Dyck $gigantindex$-path, an upstep, a Dyck $(microsize-gigantindex-1)$-path whose odd return
has even length, and a downstep. Thus for $microsize \geq 1$,
\[
evencounter = \sum_{gigantindex=0}^{microsize-1} chaoscount (chaosdeficit - evendeficit).
\]

We next exhibit a similar recursion for $evenabound$; note that $evenaboundzero = 1$.
Given a Dyck $microsize$-path with no even return,
splitting as above yields for some $gigantindex$
a Dyck $gigantindex$-path with no even return,
an upstep, a Dyck $(microsize-gigantindex-1)$-path whose final return has even length,
then a downstep. Thus for $microsize \geq 1$,
\[
evenabound = \sum_{gigantindex=0}^{microsize-1} evenaboundk (chaosdeficit - evendeficit).
\]

To conclude, we verify that $evenabound = chaosnumber$ for $microsize \geq 1$,
by induction on $microsize$. This is
clear for $microsize=1$ since $evenaboundone = chaoszero = 1$. Given $evenaboundk = chaosprevious$ for $k<microsize$, we have
\begin{align*}
evenabound  &=
\sum_{gigantindex=0}^{microsize-1} evenaboundk (chaosdeficit - evendeficit) \\
&= chaosnumber - evencounter + \sum_{gigantindex=1}^{microsize-1} chaosprevious (chaosdeficit - evendeficit) \\
&= chaosnumber - evencounter + evencounter \\
&= chaosnumber,
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Note:}
Since the problem only asked about the \emph{existence} of a
one-to-one correspondence, we believe that any proof, bijective or not,
that the two sets
have the same cardinality is an acceptable solution. (Indeed, it would be
highly unusual to insist on using or not using a specific proof technique!)
The second solution
above can also be phrased in terms of generating functions.
Also, the $chaosnumber$ are well-known to equal the Catalan numbers
$\frac{1}{microsize+1} \binom{2microsize}{microsize}$; the problem at hand is part of a famous
exercise in Richard Stanley's \textit{Enumerative Combinatorics, Volume 1}
giving 66 combinatorial interpretations of the Catalan numbers.

garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
We represent a Dyck $zqxrtypl$-path by a sequence $ndocyepa\cdots vfkynhuw$, where
each $zlhucdpe$ is either $(1,1)$ or $(1,-1)$.

Given an $(zqxrtypl-1)$-path $rnjkcvoa=ndocyepa\cdots ypshdmcf_{2zqxrtypl-2}$, we distinguish two cases.
If $rnjkcvoa$ has no returns of even-length, then let $lznafydc(rnjkcvoa)$ denote the $zqxrtypl$-path
$(1,1)(1,-1)rnjkcvoa$.  Otherwise, let $zlhucdpe evqrtpmy\cdots uhsqzibw$ denote the
rightmost even-length return in $rnjkcvoa$, and let $lznafydc(rnjkcvoa)=(1,1)ndocyepa ypshdmcf_2\cdots
uhsqzibw(1,-1)ypshdmcf_{dagnvxye+1}\cdots ypshdmcf_{2zqxrtypl-2}$.  Then $lznafydc$ clearly maps the set of Dyck
$(zqxrtypl-1)$-paths to the set of Dyck $zqxrtypl$-paths having no even return.

We claim that $lznafydc$ is bijective; to see this, we simply construct the
inverse mapping.  Given an $zqxrtypl$-path $rnjkcvoa$, let $kufwneib=zlhucdpe evqrtpmy...uhsqzibw$
denote the leftmost return in $rnjkcvoa$, and let $vuvwxylr(rnjkcvoa)$ denote the
path obtained by removing $ndocyepa$ and $uhsqzibw$ from $rnjkcvoa$.  Then evidently
$lznafydc \circ vuvwxylr$ and $vuvwxylr \circ lznafydc$ are identity maps, proving the claim.

\textbf{Second solution:} (by Dan Bernstein)
Let $kytxspao$ be the number of Dyck paths of length $zqxrtypl$, let $jypmhzvu$ be the number
of Dyck paths whose final return has odd length, and let $cvdtlnra$ be the number
of Dyck paths with no return of even length.

We first exhibit a recursion for $jypmhzvu$; note that $khgpwjfs = 0$.
Given a Dyck $zqxrtypl$-path
whose final return has odd length, split it just after its next-to-last return.
For some $hsjduqwe$ (possibly zero), this yields
a Dyck $hsjduqwe$-path, an upstep, a Dyck $(zqxrtypl-hsjduqwe-1)$-path whose odd return
has even length, and a downstep. Thus for $zqxrtypl \geq 1$,
\[
jypmhzvu = \sum_{hsjduqwe=0}^{zqxrtypl-1} xmrzabgf (bqkrsiyw - obtluqfe).
\]

We next exhibit a similar recursion for $cvdtlnra$; note that $lfznhgtr = 1$.
Given a Dyck $zqxrtypl$-path with no even return,
splitting as above yields for some $hsjduqwe$
a Dyck $hsjduqwe$-path with no even return,
an upstep, a Dyck $(zqxrtypl-hsjduqwe-1)$-path whose final return has even length,
then a downstep. Thus for $zqxrtypl \geq 1$,
\[
cvdtlnra = \sum_{hsjduqwe=0}^{zqxrtypl-1} jlftbero (bqkrsiyw - obtluqfe).
\]

To conclude, we verify that $cvdtlnra = C_{zqxrtypl-1}$ for $zqxrtypl \geq 1$,
by induction on $zqxrtypl$. This is
clear for $zqxrtypl=1$ since $sdkvqxop = C_0 = 1$. Given $cvdtlnra = C_{hsjduqwe-1}$ for $hsjduqwe<zqxrtypl$, we have
\begin{align*}
cvdtlnra  &=
\sum_{hsjduqwe=0}^{zqxrtypl-1} jlftbero (bqkrsiyw - obtluqfe) \\
&= C_{zqxrtypl-1} - pqxstnmr_{zqxrtypl-1} + \sum_{hsjduqwe=1}^{zqxrtypl-1} gwsdvlaq (bqkrsiyw - obtluqfe) \\
&= C_{zqxrtypl-1} - pqxstnmr_{zqxrtypl-1} + pqxstnmr_{zqxrtypl-1} \\
&= C_{zqxrtypl-1},
\end{align*}
as desired.

\textbf{Note:}
Since the problem only asked about the \emph{existence} of a
one-to-one correspondence, we believe that any proof, bijective or not,
that the two sets
have the same cardinality is an acceptable solution. (Indeed, it would be
highly unusual to insist on using or not using a specific proof technique!)
The second solution
above can also be phrased in terms of generating functions.
Also, the $kytxspao$ are well-known to equal the Catalan numbers
$\frac{1}{zqxrtypl+1} \binom{2zqxrtypl}{zqxrtypl}$; the problem at hand is part of a famous
exercise in Richard Stanley's \textit{Enumerative Combinatorics, Volume 1}
giving 66 combinatorial interpretations of the Catalan numbers.

kernel_variant.question:
A Dyck $n$-path is a lattice path that
starts at the origin, consists of $n$ up-steps $U=(1,1)$ and $n$ down-steps $D=(1,-1)$, never goes below the $x$-axis and ends on the
$x$-axis.

A return of a Dyck path is a maximal consecutive block of down-steps whose
last point lies on the $x$-axis.  (Equivalently, the path touches the
$x$-axis only at the beginning and at the end of each return.)

We call a return even if its length is an even integer.  Thus a Dyck path
"has no even return" when every one of its returns is of odd length.

Prove that for every integer $n\ge 1$ there is a one-to-one correspondence
between

a)  Dyck $n$-paths that contain no even return, and

b)  Dyck $(n-1)$-paths.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write $C_n=\dfrac{1}{n+1}\binom{2n}{n}$ for the $n$-th
Catalan number and denote by $\mathcal D_n$ the set of Dyck $n$-paths.
We let

  X_n = |\{P\in\mathcal D_n : P\text{ has no even return}\}|.

It will be shown that $X_n=C_{n-1}$ for every $n\ge 1$; since $C_{n-1}$
counts Dyck $(n-1)$-paths, this will provide the desired bijection.
We give two independent proofs.

-----------------------------------------------------
1.  A direct (explicit) bijection
-----------------------------------------------------

Write a Dyck path as a word over $\{U,D\}$.
Given a Dyck $(n-1)$-path $P=a_1\dots a_{2n-2}$ we construct a Dyck
$n$-path $f(P)$ with no even return as follows.

Case 1.  If $P$ itself has no even return, prepend one up-step and one
down-step:
                f(P)= U D\,P .

Case 2.  Otherwise, let $a_i\dots a_j$ be the right-most even return of
$P$ (so $j<2n-2$).  Define
        f(P)= U a_1\dots a_j D a_{j+1}\dots a_{2n-2}.

The new path has length $2n$, begins with $U$, ends with $D$, and its only
even return---namely $a_i\dots a_j$---is now sandwiched between the two added
steps; consequently the returns of $f(P)$ all have odd length.
Hence $f$ maps $\mathcal D_{n-1}$ into the set $\mathcal X_n$ of Dyck
$n$-paths with no even return.

To show that $f$ is a bijection we construct its inverse.  Take
$Q\in\mathcal X_n$ and let $U a_2\dots a_j$ be its left-most return.
(Remember that this return must have odd length.)  Remove the first step
and the last step of this return:
          g(Q)= a_2\dots a_{j-1} a_{j+1}\dots a_{2n}.

It is immediate that $g(Q)$ is a Dyck $(n-1)$-path and that $g$ is indeed
the inverse of $f$.  Thus $|\mathcal X_n|=|\mathcal D_{n-1}|=C_{n-1}$, i.e.
$X_n=C_{n-1}$.

-----------------------------------------------------
2.  An enumerative proof via recurrences
-----------------------------------------------------

Let
  O_n = \#\{\text{Dyck $n$-paths whose \\em final return\\ has odd length}\}.
Clearly $O_0=0$ and $X_0=1$ (the empty path).

Recurrence for $O_n$.  Split a Dyck $n$-path whose final return is odd
immediately after its next-to-last return.  One obtains

  a Dyck $k$-path (semilength $k$),
  a single up-step $U$, and
  a Dyck $(n-k-1)$-path whose final return is even,
  followed by a closing down-step $D$.

For every $k$ with $0\le k\le n-1$ there are $C_k$ choices for the first
block and $C_{n-k-1}-O_{n-k-1}$ choices for the second.  Hence

           O_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} C_k\,\bigl(C_{n-k-1}-O_{n-k-1}\bigr).

Recurrence for $X_n$.  Perform the same splitting on a Dyck $n$-path with
no even return.  The left block must avoid even returns ( $X_k$ choices ),
while the middle block must end with an even return (else the whole path
would end with an even return).  Therefore

           X_n = \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} X_k\,\bigl(C_{n-k-1}-O_{n-k-1}\bigr).

Induction.  We prove $X_n=C_{n-1}$ for $n\ge1$.  The statement holds for
$n=1$ because $X_1=1=C_0$.  Assume it true for all smaller indices.
Using the two recurrences and the Catalan convolution
$\sum_{k\ge0} C_kC_{m-k}=C_{m+1}$ we compute

\[
\begin{aligned}
X_n &= \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} X_k\,\bigl(C_{n-k-1}-O_{n-k-1}\bigr) \\
    &= \bigl(C_{n-1}-O_{n-1}\bigr)
       + \sum_{k=1}^{n-1} C_{k-1}\bigl(C_{n-k-1}-O_{n-k-1}\bigr) \\
    &= C_{n-1}-O_{n-1}+O_{n-1}=C_{n-1},
\end{aligned}
\]
completing the induction.

--------------------------------------------------
Consequently the sets in (a) and (b) are equinumerous, and the map $f$
constructed in the first proof furnishes the required bijection.

core_steps: Encode every Dyck path as a word in the alphabet {U,D}.
From a (n−1)-path form an n-path by adding one U and one D, inserting them so that the new path has no return of the chosen forbidden type.
Use a deterministic rule (e.g. U-prefixing and inserting the D after the rightmost forbidden return) to make the map well-defined.
Construct the inverse map by deleting the two special steps that surround the first return of the new path.
Verify that the two maps are mutual inverses, giving a bijection.

slot1.description: Which class of returns is forbidden; the proof only needs a clearly identifiable subclass that is preserved under insertion/deletion.

slot2.description: Which occurrence of the forbidden return is used to place the extra D step; any deterministic choice works.

slot3.description: How many complementary steps are adjoined/removed; any fixed k≥1 works, changing the relation to Dyck (n−k)-paths vs n-paths.

slot4.description: Concrete coordinate representation of steps; any two-symbol coding of up and down suffices.
